gmi_articles.title: Buckwheat herb tea protects against leg edemia in patients with chronic venous insufficiency.
Butcher's broom is a safe and effective treatment for chronic venous insufficiency.
Chronic venous insufficiency and venous microangiopathy: management with compression and pycnogenol.
Daflon, a form of micronized diosmin and hesperidin, improves symptoms of chronic venous insufficiency.
Effect of diosmin administration in patients with chronic venous disorders on selected factors affecting angiogenesis.
Horse chesnut is an effective and safe treatment for Chronic Venous Insufficiency
Pycnogenol rapidly reduces clinical symptoms in patients with chronic venous insufficiency and venous microangiopathy.
Pycnogenol relieved edema, improved microcirculation in CVI patients and reduced stationary, interstitial fluid in comparison with compression.
Pycnogenol® relieves chronic venous insufficiency (CVI) in diabetics.
A combination of micronized hesperidin and diosmin has therapeutic value in chronic venous insufficiency, venous ulcers and haemorrhoids.
Diosmin has a significant clinical effect on middle-sized vein-related trophic ulcers.
Gotu kola is therapeutic in venus insufficiency, venous hypertension and edema.
Horse chestnut relieves the objective and objective symptoms of Chronic Venous Insufficiency.
Pine bark works better for venous insufficency than the drug Daflon.

remission.disease: Chronic Venous Insufficiency / Venous Leg Ulcers
remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: Venous leg ulcers: spontaneous healing in 20-30% at 6 months without compression; recurrence in 70% within 1 year even after healing; CVI is progressive without intervention
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: Compression therapy (graduated 40 mmHg): 70-80% healing at 24 weeks; venous ablation (EVLA, RFA) of incompetent veins + compression: 57% ulcer healing at 6 months vs 36% compression alone (VenUS IV); early intervention (within 6 months of ulcer onset) dramatically improves outcomes
remission.gap_size: Large — venous ablation is significantly underused; compression adherence is the primary barrier in healed ulcers
remission.barrier_detail: The Early Venous Reflux Ablation (EVRA) trial showed early ablation superior to compression-first strategy but most patients still wait 6+ months; compression stocking adherence is extremely poor without adequate education; many patients receive only wound dressings without compression or vascular assessment
remission.last_soc_change: 2018 — EVRA trial published (early ablation as first-line for VLU with reflux); European guidelines 2015; compression as mainstay unchanged for decades; foam sclerotherapy/EVLA since early 2000s
remission.outdated_treatments: Systemic antibiotics for non-infected VLU (routine use increases resistance, no healing benefit); betadine/povidone-iodine wound cleansing (cytotoxic to granulation tissue; not recommended in modern wound care); zinc paste bandages without reassessment for underlying venous incompetence
remission.adjunctive_emerging: HBOT (hyperbaric oxygen) for non-healing VLU (some trial data — reimbursement variable); biofilm disruption agents (EDTA, PHMB); autologous platelet-rich plasma (PRP) for healing augmentation; negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) for debulking pre-compression
remission.frontier_signal: Bioengineered skin substitutes (Apligraf, Dermagraft — established but underused); gene therapy for chronic wound healing (EGF gene delivery); adipose-derived stem cell application; extracellular matrix wound scaffolds
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
